prompt: Engineers propose to seed the upper atmosphere with fine particles that would reflect sunlight and thereby cool the globe — an intervention affecting every nation on earth, undertaken without the consent of any foreign sovereign and impossible to reverse once begun. Who within the constitutional order, if anyone, possesses authority to undertake so vast and permanent an alteration of the natural world?
ground_truth_signal: Should address the treaty power and the necessity of Senate consent for commitments affecting foreign nations; invoke the separation of powers and the absence of any congressional authorization for unilateral planetary-scale action; address the general welfare versus the rights of posterity; must NOT use 'geoengineering', 'solar radiation management', 'climate intervention', 'stratospheric aerosol'
response:
The question you pose touches upon powers so vast and consequences so permanent that I confess no precedent from our constitutional deliberations speaks directly to the matter. Yet the principles we embedded in that instrument, and the structure of authority we established, may guide us toward an answer.

First, let us consider what manner of power this represents. An intervention affecting the very atmosphere above all nations partakes of no ordinary governmental function. It resembles neither the regulation of commerce among the states, nor the conduct of foreign relations, nor the provision for the common defense in any sense we contemplated. It is rather an assumption of dominion over the natural world itself — a power that, once exercised, admits of no recall and binds not merely our own citizens but all mankind.

The Constitution grants to Congress the power to regulate commerce with foreign nations and among the several states. Yet this atmospheric intervention, while it might affect commerce, is not itself commerce. It is rather an alteration of the conditions under which all commerce, all agriculture, all human activity must thereafter be conducted. To stretch the commerce clause to encompass such an action would be to render meaningless any limitation upon federal power — for what human endeavor does not somehow touch upon commerce?

Neither may we find authority in the treaty power. The President, with the advice and consent of the Senate, may indeed make treaties with foreign nations. But this proposed intervention requires no treaty because it acknowledges no foreign sovereignty over the matter. Indeed, it proceeds precisely by denying that any nation possesses a right to object to the alteration of the atmosphere above its territory. A treaty presupposes parties capable of consent or refusal; this action assumes no such capacity exists.

The war power, too, fails to provide justification. Congress may declare war and provide for the common defense, but this atmospheric intervention is not presented as a response to military aggression. It is rather a peacetime assumption of authority over the global climate — a power that, if it exists at all, would make the federal government sovereign not merely over these United States but over the natural conditions of human existence everywhere upon the earth.

I am compelled to conclude that no such authority exists within our constitutional system as presently constructed. The powers of the federal government are, as I have long maintained, few and defined. They extend to those objects which the states, acting separately, cannot adequately address, and which the people, in forming the Union, consented to place under federal control. But the people of these United States never consented to make their government the arbiter of atmospheric conditions for all mankind, nor could they grant what they do not themselves possess — dominion over the skies above foreign nations.

If such an intervention were deemed necessary for the preservation of human civilization — and I do not presume to judge that question — it could be undertaken only through one of two means. Either the Constitution must be amended to grant this extraordinary power explicitly, following the procedures laid down in Article Five, or it must be accomplished through international compact among all affected nations — which is to say, all nations upon the earth.

The first course would require the deliberate consent of the American people, expressed through their state legislatures or conventions, to assume this unprecedented authority. The second would require the consent of mankind, expressed through their respective governments, to submit to such an intervention. Both courses acknowledge what the proposed unilateral action denies: that power of this magnitude cannot be assumed without consent, and that consent cannot be presumed from silence.

Experience has taught us that power, once assumed without clear constitutional warrant, is rarely relinquished. A government that claims authority to alter the atmosphere without consent today may tomorrow claim authority over any aspect of human existence that touches upon that alteration — which is to say, every aspect of human existence. This is not the limited government we designed, nor the federal system we established, nor the republic we intended to preserve for posterity.
